content:
# Neural Network Batching vs. Producer-Consumer — Learning

## What "batching" means in this codebase

Batching = N frames stacked into one `(N, 3, H, W)` tensor passed through a **single forward pass**. The GPU's tensor cores execute this as one fused matrix operation across the batch dimension. It is NOT:
- Multiple parallel processes
- Multiple pipelines
- Producer-consumer threading

Example: `DEPTH_BATCH_SIZE=32` means 32 calibration frames go through Depth Anything in one `model(batch)` call. The GPU processes all 32 simultaneously.

## Why N=1 starves the GPU

The RTX 3060 has 3584 CUDA cores. A single 518×294 depth inference uses ~20% of them — the tensor is too small to saturate the hardware. At N=32 the utilisation reaches ~80%+ because the tensor cores have enough work. This is the "spiky" pattern: GPU bursts during inference, idle during IPC.

## What producer-consumer would address (different bottleneck)

Producer-consumer = CPU threading to overlap decode/compute/encode in Pass 2:
- Thread 1: ffmpeg decode → channel
- Thread 2: channel → `grade_with_grader` (GPU) → channel  
- Thread 3: channel → ffmpeg encode

This is the right optimization for the grading loop idle time (SM utilisation 7–16%, PCIe-bandwidth limited per v4 benchmark). It is NOT what inference batching addresses.

## These are orthogonal optimizations

| Technique | Bottleneck | Layer |
|---|---|---|
| Inference batching | GPU underutilisation (small tensors) | Python/torch |
| Producer-consumer | CPU/GPU sync barriers in grading loop | Rust/grade.rs |
| Fused RAUNE+depth call | IPC round-trips + unnecessary dtoh/htod | IPC boundary |
